get.summary:
Gets the latest state of a long-running operation.  Clients can use this
method to poll the operation result at intervals as recommended by the API
service.

parameters.name: name
parameters.description: The name of the operation resource.

post.summary:
Waits until the specified long-running operation is done or reaches at most
a specified timeout, returning the latest state.  If the operation is
already done, the latest state is immediately returned.  If the timeout
specified is greater than the default HTTP/RPC timeout, the HTTP/RPC
timeout is used.  If the server does not support this method, it returns
`google.rpc.Code.UNIMPLEMENTED`.
Note that this method is on a best-effort basis.  It may return the latest
state before the specified timeout (including immediately), meaning even an
immediate response is no guarantee that the operation is done.

parameters.name: name
parameters.description: The name of the operation resource to wait on.

parameters.name: timeout
parameters.description:
The maximum duration to wait before timing out. If left blank, the wait
will be at most the time permitted by the underlying HTTP/RPC protocol.
If RPC context deadline is also specified, the shorter one will be used.

JitPolicyTransactionStatusTypeMessageAcc.description:
- CREATED: Initial status of the JitPolicyRequest when it is created by the User.
The JitPolicyTransaction is created right after the JitPolicyRequest is created
with this status (as a transaction). The transaction, is initialized with
status "CREATED".
 - REQUEST_ANALYSIS_IN_PROGRESS: A JitPolicyTransaction is marked SUBMITTED once the JIT Controller reads/registers
the "CREATED" JIT Request.
The JIT Controller must be active to mark the JIT Transaction as SUBMITTED.
As part of the Controller processing, the Request is also sent for Request Analysis
by the Andromeda system.
 - REQUEST_ANALYZED: A JitPolicyTransaction is marked REQUEST_ANALYZED when the JIT Controller gets
a response from the analyzer system.
As part of the Controller processing, the Request is also assigned Reviewers
and Reviewers are notified. The Reviewers can, at this point, view the analyzed
JIT Policy Request, and approval/reject the Request.
 - REVIEW_IN_PROGRESS: JitPolicyRequest is marked REVIEW_IN_PROGRESS once initial set of Reviewers are notified.
Andromeda System then waits for the Approval/Rejection/Timeout of the Reviewers.
The Request stays in this state until all the required Reviewers have responded.
 - APPROVED: JitPolicyRequest is APPROVED once all Reviewers have APPROVED.
 - SCHEDULED_FOR_PROVISIONING: JitPolicyRequest is SCHEDULED_FOR_PROVISIONING for a future time, once it has been APPROVED.
The startTime for the Provisioning is asked as part of the JitPolicyRequest creation.
 - WAITING_FOR_MANUAL_PROVISIONING: The Request is WAITING_FOR_MANUAL_PROVISIONING to be completed, when request profile has
manual provisioning/deprovisioning enabled.
 - PROVISIONING_IN_PROGRESS: Transient
 - PROVISIONED: JIT Session is PROVISIONED and the User can access the resources.
After the Provisioning, the Request is scheduled for Deprovisioning.
 - DEPROVISIONING_IN_PROGRESS: Transient
 - WAITING_FOR_MANUAL_DEPROVISIONING: The Request is WAITING_FOR_MANUAL_DEPROVISIONING to be completed, when request profile has
manual provisioning/deprovisioning enabled.
 - DEPROVISIONED: JIT Session is DEPROVISIONED and the User can no longer access the resources.
After the successful DeProvisioning, the JIT Controller schedules a check to validate whether
the Policy has been Deprovisioned for the User.
 - SESSION_ANALYSIS_PENDING: Once the JIT Controller detects a deprovisioned action, the Session is marked
as pending for analysis by the Session summarizer.
 - CANCELLATION_IN_PROGRESS: Access request status is marked as CANCELLATION_IN_PROGRESS when:
1. The Requester manually ends the session. Here the controller will be cleaning up
  running processes for the request.
 - REJECTED: JitPolicyRequest is REJECTED once a Reviewer rejects the Request.
This is a terminal state.
 - DEPROVISIONING_FAILED: JIT Request is marked as DEPROVISIONING_FAILED when the deprovisioning fails.
Deprovisioning can be retried using the bulk retrigger deprovisioning API.
 - PROVISIONING_FAILED: JIT Request is marked as PROVISIONING_FAILED when the provisioning fails.
Provisioning can be retried using the bulk retrigger provisioning API.
 - FAILED: JIT processing failed during any of the previous steps.
This is a terminal state.
 - TIMED_OUT: Request is Time out when start time passes and review is not approved.
This is a terminal state.
 - CLOSED: Request is closed before PROVISIONED.
This is a terminal state.
 - COMPLETED: JitPolicyRequest is marked as COMPLETED from either:
1. The Requester manually ending the Session after it is Provisioned, or
2. The Controller archiving it once the Session has been analyzed in the end, or
This is a terminal state.

 - POLICY_RECOMMENDATION_TYPE_EXPAND_LOWRISK: Read metadata flow - includes used permissions plus List/Read/ReadMetadata permissions for services used in the past

PolicySignificanceTypeMessagePolicySigni.title:
- USED_BY_MULTIPLE_NON_HUMAN_IDENTITIES: Policies that are used by multiple non-human identities.
Category: POSTURE
 - UNUSED_30_90_DAYS: These are policies that are not used by any of the identities in the account
In the Policy Details page it will have zero used.
From grounding perpective - need to show the list of the policies in the account
that are not used and for each one we can show the policy details.
As high priority only implement the unused policies.
Category: ACTIVITY
 - NOT_ALL_ASSIGNMENTS_USED_365_DAYS_PLUS: Policies that have only used by some of the identities who have access to the account
These assignements can be removed from the identity. Note, when assignment is via
group then group members need to be identified who needs to be removed from the list.
PARTIAL_ASSIGNMENTS_USED
These are computed based on unused of
Category: POSTURE
 - OVERPROVISIONED: These policies are overprovisioned in the account.
It is basically used/total permissions < 10 %.
These are tagged only for High Risk Policies. I am divided as we can always
combine high blast risk with overprovisioned and get the same result.
Category: PRIVILEGE
 - HAS_IMPROPER_TRUST: Policies having self trusted roles
 - ADMIN_PRIVILEDGE: Policies that have admin permissions.
Category: PRIVILEGE

email.description:
Email can either be of a SSO User, or a non-SSO User. The type of
User (SSO/non-SSO) is determined by checking the domain in the Email.
A non-SSO User is logged in via an OTP to the Email.
An SSO User on the other hand is logged in by redirecting it to
the configured IdP.

returnUrl.description:
ReturnURL is an optional setting for the API Client, that sets
the URL to which the User should return to after a successful
SAML Login. This applies for when the Email is that of an SSO-User.

apiLoginRequest.description:

on the login page.

apiLoginScheme.description:
- LOGIN_SCHEME_EMAIL_OTP: OTP is the default login scheme for non-SSO Users.

email.description:
Email is the email of the User who wishes to access Andromeda resources.
Email is optional in AccessKeyLogin, and required in EmailLogin and SAMLLogin.

code.description:
Code is determined by what authentication flow is being used.
If the Login is initiated via the OTP flow, the code is the OTP sent on mail.
If the Login is initiated via the SAML flow, the code is what the IdP provides
post the redirects.
IF the Login is via the Access key flow, the code is the generated access key.

assignReviewersTemplate.description:
AssignReviewersTemplate represents the chain of approvers in the intender order,
with the first set of reviewers assigned from the first index and so on.
It'll allow the reviewer configuration to look something like this:
assign_reviewers_template: [
  {persona: IDENTITY, persona_values: ["user1", "user2", "user3"], required_approvals: 2},
  {persona: GROUP_MEMBER, persona_values: ["group1"], assignable_reviewers: 3, required_approvals: 1},
  {persona: MANAGER, required_approvals: 1},
  {persona: MANAGER, required_approvals: 1},
  {persona: ACCOUNT_OWNER, assignable_reviewers: 2, required_approvals: 1},
]
Manager has a special handling which is when a manager follows a manager, it is
intended to go to the manager of the previous manager.
DEPERECATED.

matchNameValue.description:
In case the template variable does not correspond to a valid value in the JIT context,
For instance if JIT is at an Account level but the template provides a resourceGroup variable,
The resourceGroup variable will be replaced with an empty string.

Example usage:
my-prefix-{{accountName}}-{{policyName}}--{{resourceGroupId}}
In this example, if accountName is "exampleAccount", policyName is "examplePolicy", and
resourceGroupId is "rg-12345", the resolved string would be:
my-prefix-exampleAccount-examplePolicy--rg-12345
This is the group that will be used to provision an access request for
Polcy `examplePolicy` at a ResourceGroup `rg-12345``, in Account `exampleAccount`.
matchNameValue.title:
MatchNameValue used to specify a string template that allows dynamic insertion of
various parameters related to accounts, policies, and resource groups.
This field accepts a templated string format where specific variables will be
substituted with their corresponding values at runtime - while provisioning / deprovisioning.
Supported template variables are:
{{accountId}}, {{accountName}}
{{policyId}}, {{policyName}} and
{{resourceGroupId}}, {{resourceGroupName}}

isPasswordEncrypted.description:
When true, the value in `password_env_var_name` is expected to be produced
by the broker `encrypt` command and will be decrypted at runtime.

To encrypt a plaintext password on the broker host:
  - Linux/macOS: ./andromeda-broker encrypt --password "<plaintext>"
  - Windows:     andromeda-broker.exe encrypt --password "<plaintext>"
  - Interactive (no shell history exposure):
      ./andromeda-broker encrypt
      andromeda-broker.exe encrypt

Then set the environment variable named in `password_env_var_name` to the output in your `.env` file and set `is_password_encrypted = true`.

configPatternMatchCriteriaMessageCriteri.description:
Enum to specify the type of matching criteria.

 - UNSPECIFIED: Default value indicating no specific criteria set. This will be treated as an error.
 - SUFFIX: Matches if the target ends with the specified value.
 - PREFIX: Matches if the target starts with the specified value.
 - EXACT: Matches if the target is an exact match with the specified value.
 - REGEX: For example, the normal user is kamal.murali@andromedasecurity.com and the privileged user is
“su-kamal.murali@andromedasecurity.com”. In this case after you remove the prefix, the username
is an exact match to the HI username. The normal user is kamal.murali@andromedasecurity.com and
the privileged user is “su-kmurali@andromedasecurity.com” . Now we need to form the privileged
user username using first letter of first name appended with last name with a prefix of “su-”

configPolicyTypeMessagePolicyType.title:
- AWS_PERMISSION_SET: AWS Permission Set in AWS Identity Center
 - AWS_IAM_ROLE: AWS Role in IAM
 - AWS_USER_PERMISSIONS: AWS User-level permission policies
 - AWS_GROUP_PERMISSIONS: AWS Group-level permission policies
 - GCP_ROLE: GCP Role
 - WORKSPACE_ROLE: Workspace role
 - WORKSPACE_OAUTH_OBO_POLICY: Workspace OAuth OBO (On Behalf Of) policy - represents OAuth scopes granted to third-party apps
 - AZURE_ROLE: Azure role

 - AWS_ROOT: AWS Root policy

from the IDP to the provider. (shadow of a push group in app-bound provider)

name.description:
The server-assigned name, which is only unique within the same service that
originally returns it. If you use the default HTTP mapping, the
`name` should be a resource name ending with `operations/{unique_id}`.

metadata.description:
Service-specific metadata associated with the operation.  It typically
contains progress information and common metadata such as create time.
Some services might not provide such metadata.  Any method that returns a
long-running operation should document the metadata type, if any.

done.description:
If the value is `false`, it means the operation is still in progress.
If `true`, the operation is completed, and either `error` or `response` is
available.

error.description: The error result of the operation in case of failure or cancellation.

response.description:
The normal response of the operation in case of success.  If the original
method returns no data on success, such as `Delete`, the response is
`google.protobuf.Empty`.  If the original method is standard
`Get`/`Create`/`Update`, the response should be the resource.  For other
methods, the response should have the type `XxxResponse`, where `Xxx`
is the original method name.  For example, if the original method name
is `TakeSnapshot()`, the inferred response type is
`TakeSnapshotResponse`.

googlelongrunningOperation.description:
This resource represents a long-running operation that is the result of a

code.description:
The status code, which should be an enum value of
[google.rpc.Code][google.rpc.Code].

message.description:
A developer-facing error message, which should be in English. Any
user-facing error message should be localized and sent in the
[google.rpc.Status.details][google.rpc.Status.details] field, or localized

googlerpcStatus.description:

different programming environments, including REST APIs and RPC APIs. It is
used by [gRPC](https://github.com/grpc). Each `Status` message contains
three pieces of data: error code, error message, and error details.

You can find out more about this error model and how to work with it in the
[API Design Guide](https://cloud.google.com/apis/design/errors).

graphBucketOptions.description:
`BucketOptions` describes the bucket boundaries used to create a histogram
for the distribution. The buckets can be in a linear sequence, an
exponential sequence, or each bucket can be specified explicitly.
`BucketOptions` does not include the number of values in each bucket.

A bucket has an inclusive lower bound and exclusive upper bound for the
values that are counted for that bucket. The upper bound of a bucket must
be strictly greater than the lower bound. The sequence of N buckets for a
distribution consists of an underflow bucket (number 0), zero or more
finite buckets (number 1 through N - 2) and an overflow bucket (number N -
1). The buckets are contiguous: the lower bound of bucket i (i > 0) is the
same as the upper bound of bucket i - 1. The buckets span the whole range
of finite values: lower bound of the underflow bucket is -infinity and the
upper bound of the overflow bucket is +infinity. The finite buckets are
so-called because both bounds are finite.

graphCrossAccountInfo.description:
[More complex case] user-A has PSet-1 in account-A and Pset-2 in account-B.
So it would show up as "self" in account-B's identity list.
But also has lateral access from account-A (role-abe in account-B has trust to PSet-A in account-A).
Do we need to reflect both cases of "Self" and cross? It could have cross from multiple accounts.
Do we need to reflect it? IdentityOriginAccountId should reflect [account-A, account-B]
No issue with Service-Role is it can only be in one account.
Challenges with IAM user (which we classify as human and merge if username is same in multiple accounts).
iam-user foo exists in a-A + a-B + a-Z. In account C there are two roles - r-1 with trust to a-A/foo and r-2 with trust to a-B/foo.
In addition, (no trust for a-Z/foo for any role) in Account-C's human
list we must list foo as cross and origin-account needs to be repeated and list a-A, a-B
graphCrossAccountInfo.title:
IAM user (or service role) in account-A has a direct trust to a role in Account-B Complex cases
Identity Center user-A has PSet-1 in account-A (and not pset binding for account-B).
Role abc in Account-B has trust to PSet-A. So user-A has via lateral access to account-B.
So in Account-B's list of identities, we need to reflect user-A

bucketOptions.description:
Defines the histogram bucket boundaries. If the distribution does not
contain a histogram, then omit this field.

bucketCounts.description:
The number of values in each bucket of the histogram, as described in
`bucket_options`. If the distribution does not have a histogram, then omit
this field. If there is a histogram, then the sum of the values in
`bucket_counts` must equal the value in the `count` field of the
distribution.

If present, `bucket_counts` should contain N values, where N is the number
of buckets specified in `bucket_options`. If you supply fewer than N
values, the remaining values are assumed to be 0.

The order of the values in `bucket_counts` follows the bucket numbering
schemes described for the three bucket types. The first value must be the
count for the underflow bucket (number 0). The next N-2 values are the
counts for the finite buckets (number 1 through N-2). The N'th value in
`bucket_counts` is the count for the overflow bucket (number N-1).

policyAccessRequestProfile.description:
policyAccessRequestProfile holds the effective access request profile details for this resource group.
It is highly advised to request for this field only for a single policy at a time,
because gathering effective profiles for multiple policies at a time could be expensive.

bounds.description: The values must be monotonically increasing.

graphExplicit.description:
Specifies a set of buckets with arbitrary widths.

There are `size(bounds) + 1` (= N) buckets. Bucket `i` has the following
boundaries:

   Upper bound (0 <= i < N-1):     bounds[i]
   Lower bound (1 <= i < N);       bounds[i - 1]

The `bounds` field must contain at least one element. If `bounds` has
only one element, then there are no finite buckets, and that single
element is the common boundary of the overflow and underflow buckets.

graphExponential.description:
Specifies an exponential sequence of buckets that have a width that is
proportional to the value of the lower bound. Each bucket represents a
constant relative uncertainty on a specific value in the bucket.

There are `num_finite_buckets + 2` (= N) buckets. Bucket `i` has the
following boundaries:

   Upper bound (0 <= i < N-1):     scale * (growth_factor ^ i).

   Lower bound (1 <= i < N):       scale * (growth_factor ^ (i - 1)).

fieldMask.description:
field_mask is used to set only certain fields of the request.
If it is to empty, all fields are set.

updatedAtCheck.description:
if the updated_at of the request protobuf matches this value for the primary entity.

If any egress field changes for the primary entity, the secondary entities may still be created.

useTestRegistry.description: Use the test registry. This is only used for unit testing protographs.

updateWithoutVersionCheck.description:

fieldTypeMapping.title:
field_type_mapping allows specifying concrete types for abstract node fields.
This is useful when the request contains abstract nodes (like Scope, Principal) 
and you want to specify the concrete implementation type for specific fields.
Key: field name (e.g., "scope_id", "principal_id")
Value: concrete type name (e.g., "Account", "User")
Example: {"scope_id": "Account", "principal_id": "User"}

skipSetDeletedAt.description:
Skip set_deleted_at will not reset the deletedAt field to 0 during upsert operations.
When true, the deletedAt field will remain unchanged, allowing force updates without
resurrecting deleted entities. Only applicable for UPSERT operations.

nonpublicGraphDatagram.description:
GraphDatagram encapsulates a request that has been made over a model-agnostic Graph protobuf.

When working with abstract nodes (like Scope, Principal), the system needs to know
which concrete implementation type to use for specific fields. This is achieved through
the field_type_mapping field, which maps field names to concrete types.

Example usage:
- If you have a request with a "scope_id" field that should be treated as an Account
- Set field_type_mapping: {"scope_id": CONCRETE_TYPE_ACCOUNT}
- This tells the system to treat the scope_id as an Account node type
- Similarly for "principal_id": CONCRETE_TYPE_USER to treat it as a User node

This is particularly useful when:
- The request protobuf uses abstract types but you need concrete behavior
- You want to ensure proper edge creation and validation
- You need to disambiguate between multiple possible concrete types

nonpublicGraphDatagramArrayUpsertStrateg.description:
- BLIND_MERGE: This creates MERGE…SET for each object in the array
This is the default (also, v1) strategy
 - CONDITIONAL_MERGE: Conditional MERGE does a MATCH for the object and properties
if no match is found, it does a MERGE…SET
 - CONDITIONAL_CREATE: Conditional CREATE does a match for the object and properties
if object exists but with different property set, it does a MATCH…SET
if object doesn’t exist, it does a CREATE…SET

orchestratorKubernetesInvocation.description:
TEMPLATE mode (from_cronjob): Derives the Job from an existing CronJob deployed via
  Helm. Knot reads the CronJob's .spec.jobTemplate and overrides the container args.
  All operational config (resources, affinity, labels, secrets) stays in Helm.
  Use for established executors that already have a Helm chart (e.g., lsp-eps, risk).

  INLINE mode (inline): Self-contained Job spec defined entirely in this message.
  Knot builds the Job from scratch. Use for new services that don't have a Helm chart,
  or for ad-hoc one-off jobs.

id.title: Unique identifier for the schedule

workflowDefinitionId.title: Foreign key to the workflow definition (template) that will be instantiated

tenantId.title:
Tenant ID - the tenant context for workflow instances created from this schedule
Optional for global schedules (when policy.is_global = true)
Required for tenant-specific schedules

providerId.title:
Provider ID - the provider context for workflow instances created from this schedule
Optional for global schedules (when policy.is_global = true) and tenant-wide workflows
Required for provider-specific schedules

policy.title:
Schedule policy defining execution mode and overlap handling
Required field that determines scheduling behavior

cronExpression.title:
Cron expression for TIME_BASED mode (e.g., "0 */6 * * *" for every 6 hours)
Required when policy.mode = TIME_BASED
Ignored when policy.mode = AUTO_CAPACITY
Uses standard cron format: minute hour day month weekday

enabled.title:
Whether the schedule is currently active
False = paused (won't trigger), True = active
Allows pausing schedules without deletion

nextExecutionAt.title:
Pre-calculated next execution time for efficient polling
Updated after each execution or when schedule is modified
Indexed for fast "get schedules ready to run" queries

maxExecutions.title:
Maximum number of times this schedule should execute
0 or NULL = unlimited executions (default for recurring schedules)
> 0 = schedule will stop after this many executions
Example: Set to 7 for a daily backup that runs for one week
Works with execution_count to track progress

executionCount.title:
Counter tracking how many times this schedule has been triggered
Used for monitoring, debugging, and enforcing max_executions
Reset to 0 when schedule is created or manually reset

orchestratorSchedule.title:
Schedule defines a scheduled workflow execution
Supports both auto-capacity scheduling and time-based (cron) scheduling

mode.title: Scheduling mode: AUTO_CAPACITY (knot decides when based on capacity) or TIME_BASED (runs at specific time)

overlapPolicy.title: Policy for handling overlapping executions when schedule triggers while previous run is still executing

timezone.title:
Timezone for cron evaluation (IANA timezone name, e.g., "America/New_York", "UTC", "Asia/Kolkata")
Defaults to "UTC" if not specified or empty
Only applies to FIXED_TIME mode
The cron expression will be evaluated in this timezone to determine next execution time

runImmediately.title:
If true, triggers an immediate execution when schedule is first created/enabled
After the immediate execution, follows normal cron schedule
Useful for "populate now, then keep updated" scenarios
Only applies to FIXED_TIME mode

isGlobal.title:
If true, this is a global schedule that operates across all tenants
Global schedules do not require tenant_id or provider_id
Used for system-wide operations like query cache, cleanup jobs, etc.
When true, tenant_id and provider_id in Schedule message are ignored/optional

frequency.title:
Schedule frequency: DAILY_ONCE, HOURLY, EVERY_6_HOURS, etc.
This is a higher-level abstraction that can be translated to cron expressions
If UNSPECIFIED, the schedule will use the existing mechanism

forceFullSync.title:
If true, workflows triggered by this schedule will perform a full sync
instead of incremental sync. This resets all resources to INITIATED state
and re-fetches all data from the provider.
Default: false (incremental sync)

orchestratorSchedulePolicy.title: SchedulePolicy defines how the schedule should be executed and handled

executionId.description:
Stable identifier assigned by the remote service for this execution.
Knot stores this and passes it back in subsequent GetExecutionStatus and
CancelExecution calls.

providerId.title: Provider ID for the task (optional)

name.title: Task name (must be unique within the workflow)

needs.title: Task dependencies - list of task names that must complete before this task

retryWaitSeconds.title:
Retry configuration - constant wait time between retries (in seconds)
Default: 0 (no retry wait, immediate requeue)

maxRetries.title:
Maximum number of retry attempts before marking as FAILED
Default: 0 (no retries allowed)

allowPartialDependencyFailure.title:
Allow partial dependency failure - if true, task can run if at least one dependency succeeds
If false (default), ALL dependencies must succeed for task to run
Use case: Tenant logical layer should run even if some provider workflows fail

consumeDependencyResults.description:
When true, knot collects results from dependency tasks
and injects them into this task's invocation args as DependencyResult_<task_name> keys.

timeRangeAware.description:
When true, knot looks up the most recent COMPLETED run of this (type, tenant, provider)
and injects its start_time as LastSuccessfulStartTime + CurrentRunStartTime.
Only set this for tasks that do incremental/time-range queries (delta sync, etc.).

fanOut.description:
Fan-out configuration: controls how this template task expands into N concrete tasks.
When unset (nil), the task is a single concrete task with no expansion.

disabled.description:
When true, the generic composer skips this task during expansion.
Downstream tasks that depended on it lose that dependency but still run.

orchestratorTaskInvocationDetails.title:

workflowName.title: The name of the Temporal workflow function to execute (e.g., "InventoryFetchWorkflow")

workflowArgs.title:

timeoutMinutes.title:
Timeout for the Temporal workflow execution in minutes
This sets the WorkflowExecutionTimeout when invoking the workflow
Default: 720 (12 hours) if not specified or 0

priority.title: Default priority for workflow instances created from this definition

requiredFeatureFlags.description:

This is the reusable task blueprint that gets instantiated as Task when workflow runs

@type.description:
A URL/resource name that uniquely identifies the type of the serialized
protocol buffer message. This string must contain at least
one "/" character. The last segment of the URL's path must represent
the fully qualified name of the type (as in
`path/google.protobuf.Duration`). The name should be in a canonical form
(e.g., leading "." is not accepted).

In practice, teams usually precompile into the binary all types that they
expect it to use in the context of Any. However, for URLs which use the
scheme `http`, `https`, or no scheme, one can optionally set up a type
server that maps type URLs to message definitions as follows:

* If no scheme is provided, `https` is assumed.
* An HTTP GET on the URL must yield a [google.protobuf.Type][]
  value in binary format, or produce an error.
* Applications are allowed to cache lookup results based on the
  URL, or have them precompiled into a binary to avoid any
  lookup. Therefore, binary compatibility needs to be preserved
  on changes to types. (Use versioned type names to manage
  breaking changes.)

Note: this functionality is not currently available in the official
protobuf release, and it is not used for type URLs beginning with
type.googleapis.com. As of May 2023, there are no widely used type server
implementations and no plans to implement one.

Schemes other than `http`, `https` (or the empty scheme) might be
used with implementation specific semantics.

protobufNullValue.description:
